did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework for skin tumor diagnosis using pathologic slide images. It utilized Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically comparing Dual path network, EfficientNetB6, Xception, and InceptionV3) for patch-wise feature extraction and classification, and an Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN) for slide-wise diagnosis. The study targeted Neurofibromas (NF), Bowen disease (BD), and seborrheic keratosis (SK).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a 'two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework' for skin tumor diagnosis. It details specific methods including data collection (504 slides), preprocessing, network architectures (EfficientNetB6, AGGN), and training settings. It reports original results with performance metrics (accuracy, AUC) on a specific dataset, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Two-Stage End-to-End Deep Learning Framework for Pathologic Examination in Skin Tumor Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study developed a two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework for the pathologic diagnosis of neurofibromas, Bowen disease, and seborrheic keratosis using whole-slide images. The framework utilized EfficientNetB6 for patch-wise feature extraction and an Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN) for slide-wise diagnosis on a dataset of 504 slides from Huashan Hospital. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and receiver operating characteristic curves on a held-out testing set. The model achieved high classification accuracies of 92.64% for Bowen disease, 96.88% for neurofibroma, and 99.21% for seborrheic keratosis. The results demonstrate that this deep learning approach can effectively distinguish these skin tumors and improve the efficiency of pathologic examination.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary skin tumors targeted for diagnosis in the study. Included in the dataset of 504 slides (97 total, 32 in testing set). Achieved 96.88% test accuracy and 0.99457 AUC.
One of the three primary skin tumors targeted for diagnosis. Included in the dataset of 504 slides (145 total, 68 in testing set). Achieved 92.64% test accuracy and 0.97854 AUC.
One of the three primary skin tumors targeted for diagnosis. Included in the dataset of 504 slides (196 total, 127 in testing set). Achieved 99.21% test accuracy and 0.99721 AUC.
Negative control class representing healthy tissue or absence of the target tumors. Included in the dataset of 504 slides (66 total, 50 in testing set). Achieved 86.00% test accuracy and 0.99087 AUC.

all_task_types.target: Slide-level diagnosis of skin tumors (Neurofibroma, Bowen disease, Seborrheic keratosis) and negative samples (no disease).
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a two-stage framework for 'pathologic diagnosis' and 'skin tumor classification'. The first stage performs patch-wise classification to extract features, and the second stage performs slide-wise classification using an Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN) and post-processing to predict the final slide label. The task is explicitly defined as distinguishing between discrete diagnostic categories (NF, BD, SK, and negative/IF). Performance was evaluated using accuracy and AUC (Receiver Operating Characteristic curves), which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Huashan Hospital Pathologic Slide Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of 504 whole-slide images (WSIs) collected from the Department of Dermatology at Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, Shanghai, China. The slides were scanned at 40x magnification using a NanoZoomer NDP Scan C10730-12 scanner. Lesions were manually annotated by professional pathologists.

use.training: Used to train the two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework, specifically for patch-wise feature extraction using CNNs (EfficientNetB6, Xception, etc.) and slide-wise diagnosis using the Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN).
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance during training, select the best model, and tune hyperparameters (e.g., post-processing thresholds).
use.testing: Used as a held-out set to evaluate the final classification performance (accuracy and AUC) of the proposed framework on unseen data.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the model for each class (Neurofibroma, Bowen disease, Seborrheic keratosis, and no disease).

statistical_methods_used.method: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the discriminatory ability of the model for each class, with specific values reported (e.g., 0.99721 for SK, 0.97854 for BD).
To measure the overall classification performance on the training, validation, and testing sets.
To visualize the discrimination in the embedding feature space and assess the separation of classes.
To reduce overfitting by modifying the true probability distribution during loss calculation.
To improve robustness and achieve slide-level prediction by applying thresholds for negative ratio (alpha) and false negative ratio (beta) to patch-level predictions.
To train the neural networks by calculating the difference between predicted probabilities and ground truth labels.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). The term 'significant' was used only to describe negligible changes in evaluation indices (<0.0001) during model training convergence, not as part of a statistical hypothesis test.

EfficientNet_b6.note: Outperformed Xception (94.73%), Resnet50 (91.08%), InceptionV3 (95.61%), and DPN68 (90.62%) on validation set.

CMCNN (Chain Memory Convolutional Neural.note: AGGN outperformed CMCNN on both training and validation sets.

Huashan Hospital Pathologic Slide Datase.notes: The study utilized a two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework. The first stage used EfficientNetB6 for patch-wise feature extraction. The second stage used AGGN for slide-wise diagnosis. Post-processing with statistical thresholds (negative ratio alpha and false negative ratio beta set to 3%) was applied. The model achieved high classification accuracies and AUC values on the held-out testing set.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework consisting of a first-stage Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for patch-wise feature extraction and a second-stage Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN) for slide-wise aggregation. EfficientNetB6 was selected as the primary CNN backbone. CMCNN (Chain Memory Convolutional Neural Network) was also evaluated as an alternative second-stage aggregator.
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNetB6, Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and Dual Path Network were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Whole-slide images (WSIs) scanned at 40x magnification. Slides were split into fixed-size patches. Feature map size from the first stage was 576 x 16 x 16.

first_stage_cnn_backbones: Dual Path Network (ResNet + DenseNet fused via RNN)

second_stage_aggregators.name: AGGN (Attention Graph Gated Network)
second_stage_aggregators.details: Uses graph convolutional networks. Input includes node feature X (embedding from first stage) and adjacency matrix A. Adjacency defined by Euclidean distance similarity (threshold < 0.5). Contains two attention convolution networks.

second_stage_aggregators.name: CMCNN (Chain Memory Convolutional Neural Network)
second_stage_aggregators.details: Composed of three blocks: reusable ConvLSTM layer, batch normalization layer, and maximum merge layer. ConvLSTM uses 3x3 convolution kernel and ReLU activation. Feature channels: 576, 288, 144. Followed by dropout, flatten, and softmax.

first_stage.learning_rate_schedule: Noise limit percentage 0.67, SD 1.0, warm-up LR 0.0001, lower bound 1e-5, fading period 30, warm-up period 3, cooling period 10, patience 10, attenuation rate 0.1. Converged to 0.5e-6.
first_stage.regularization: Label smoothing (q=0.1, K=1000), Training interpolation of random weights, Moving average of model weights (0.9998)

second_stage_cmcnn.learning_rate: Started at 0.001, decreased by sqrt(0.1) after 30 epochs

classification_or_output_head.first_stage: Global pooling followed by classification layer (Softmax)
classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Statistical thresholds applied to patch-level predictions: negative ratio (alpha) and false negative ratio (beta) set to 3%.

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping slides into fixed-size patches (512x512 pixels) with a stride of 256 using a sliding window
Tissue boundary detection using Otsu thresholding
Color normalization: Conversion from RGB to HSV space followed by standard regularization to normalize colors
Data augmentation via enhanced color jitter of 0.4
Random shuffling of images for dataset splitting
Manual annotation of lesions by professional pathologists under low-power magnifying lens

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training with a value of 0.4.

conclusions: The study investigated the feasibility of skin tumor diagnosis from pathologic images and may be the first instance of applying deep learning to address the diagnosis of neurofibromas, Bowen disease, and seborrheic keratosis in skin pathology.

A two-stage end-to-end deep learning framework consisting of patch-wise diagnosis and slide-wise diagnosis was proposed to improve the efficiency of pathologic examination, which is currently laborious and time-consuming.
EfficientNetB6 outperformed other classic backbone networks (Xception, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DPN68) in patch-level classification, achieving the highest validation accuracy of 95.78%.

The Attention Graph Gated Network (AGGN) was adopted to aggregate patch-level features for whole-slide level prediction, effectively fusing information from feature-embedding learning and domain knowledge.
The proposed framework achieved high classification accuracies on the testing set: 92.64% for Bowen disease, 96.88% for neurofibroma, 99.21% for seborrheic keratosis, and 86.00% for negative samples.

The model demonstrated strong discriminatory ability with Area Under the Curve (AUC) values of 0.99457 for neurofibroma, 0.97854 for Bowen disease, 0.99721 for seborrheic keratosis, and 0.99087 for negative samples.
Post-processing methods using statistical thresholds (negative ratio and false negative ratio) were used to improve the robustness of slide-wise diagnosis.
The framework is extendable and can be trained in end-to-end mode to address other skin tumors.
t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) analysis visualized clear distinction between features of the three skin tumors, indicating the advantages of the method.
Gradient-weighted class activation mapping showed consistency between pathologists and the final model prediction regarding key feature locations.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small, single-center dataset (504 slides) collected exclusively from Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, raising concerns about external validity and generalizability to other populations or institutions.

There is a significant class imbalance in the testing set, particularly for Neurofibroma (32 slides) and negative samples (50 slides), compared to Seborrheic keratosis (127 slides), which may bias performance metrics.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare the proposed framework against baseline methods or to assess the significance of performance differences.

The dataset consists of digitized pathologic slides scanned at 40x magnification using a specific scanner (NanoZoomer NDP Scan C10730-12); performance may vary with different scanners or staining protocols.

The claim that this 'may be the first instance' of applying deep learning to these specific three tumor types in skin pathology requires verification against prior literature not included in the provided context.
The model relies on manual annotations by professional pathologists for training and validation, introducing potential inter-observer variability and dependency on expert labeling quality.

Post-processing thresholds (negative ratio alpha and false negative ratio beta) were tuned on the validation set (beta set to 3% for highest validation accuracy), which may lead to optimistic performance estimates if not validated on a completely independent external cohort.
The study focuses only on four classes (Neurofibroma, Bowen disease, Seborrheic keratosis, and no disease), limiting its clinical applicability to the broader differential diagnosis of skin tumors.
